Chapter 11
Dental disease
Vladimír Jekl
Dental disease is frequently diagnosed in guinea pigs 
(Böhmer, 2015; Lennox et al., 2020) but the exact inci-
dence is disputed, with three separate studies reporting 
the incidence of oral cavity disease as 12%, 23.4% and 
36.3% (Jekl et al., 2008; Langenecker et al., 2009; 
Minarikova et al., 2015). In the last two decades, the num-
ber of articles and books describing dental disease and 
its therapy has increased. However, there are still oral 
cavity disorders that have not been precisely described 
and therapeutic approaches that are only supported by 
anecdotal evidence.
The management of oral cavity disease starts with the 
establishment of the primary cause of the disorder, which 
is informed by history, clinical examination, intraoral exami-
nation and the use of conventional and advanced imaging 
techniques, especially oral cavity endoscopy and com-
puted tomography (CT). The optimal treatment can then 
be identified, and the short-term and long-term prognosis 
can be determined. Appropriate management decisions 
are then based on discussions with the client to explain 
possible surgical and/or medical procedures. If an optimal

Guinea pigs have one pair of maxillary incisors and one 
pair of mandibular incisors. The mandibular incisors are 
much shorter. The labial surface of the incisors is, in con-
trast with chinchillas and degus, white in colour. The 
enamel is thickest on the lingual surface and thins as it 
extends to the distal and mesial surfaces. The labial sur-
face is covered with dentin and cementum. This configu-
ration of dental tissues gives a sharp chisel-like occlusion. 
About one-third of the incisor protrudes into the oral 
cavity, while two-thirds sits in the alveolus. The apices of 
guinea pig maxillary incisors extend to the mesial aspect 
of the fourth premolar and those of the mandibular inci-
sors reach to the level of the first mandibular molar 
(Berkovitz, 1972). A large diastema separates the incisor

Patient positioning and preparation
Preoperative radiographic examination or CT scans (Figure 11.1.1) are required before the procedure. The procedure 
T
is performed on the stable guinea pig under general anaesthesia and appropriate analgesia (see Chapter 7) and 
E begins with the patient in lateral recumbency. The patient is intubated or ventilated by mask. Intravenous fluid therapy 
C and patient monitoring during the anaesthesia are standard parts of a surgical procedure. The surgical field is shaved 
and prepared for surgery as standard.

Assistant
Not required.
Equipment
In addition to the basic surgical equipment common to all procedures (see Chapter 12), this procedure requires:
■  Scalpel blade No.12 or 15
■  Periosteal elevator
■  1–2 mm incisor luxator
■  Cotton tips (sterile)
■  Gauze (sterile)
■  Dental unit or piezosurgical unit
■  Self-retaining ring retractor.
Surgical technique
1  Perform regional anaesthesia (mandibular nerve block on the affected side; see Chapter 7).
2  Locate the apex of the affected tooth, which can be palpated as bone protrusion on the lateral aspect of the mandible:
a.  If a clinical crown of the affected incisor is present, disinfect the gingiva surrounding the incisor
b.  Cut the gingival attachment on all four sides of the affected incisor with a scalpel blade
c.   Gently luxate the tooth with a 1–2 mm incisor luxator. The luxation should be performed very gently and on all the tooth 
aspects.
3  Rostral mandibulotomy:
a.  Place the patient in dorsal recumbency (Figure 11.1.2)
b.  Include the clinical crown of the mandibular incisor in the surgical field
c.   Make an approximately 2 cm long incision in the skin slightly lateral to the mandibular symphysis. If an abscess is present, 
aspirate its contents using a 21 G needle, incise the skin and abscess capsule and debride the content. Do not perforate 
the oral gingiva rostrally
d.  Place a self-retaining ring retractor
e.  Using a periosteal elevator, remove the periosteum from the area where the bone will be removed
f.  R emove the bone ventral to the alveolus of the affected incisor, directly above the incisor, and follow the alveolus 
caudally
g.   Sever the periodontal ligament with a hypodermic needle, a scalpel blade or a premolar/molar tooth luxator for rabbits/
rodents. Work along the lingual, buccal and labial tooth aspect.

Patient positioning and preparation
Preoperative radiographic examination or computed tomography scans are required before the procedure (Figure 
11.2.1). As there is concurrent dental disease and especially if the guinea pig has trouble eating, it is often necessary 
to provide nutritional support via syringe feeding with an appropriate recovery diet (see Chapter 8). The procedure 
is performed on the stable guinea pig under general anaesthesia and appropriate analgesia (see Chapter 7) with the 
patient in a lateral recumbency. The patient is intubated or ventilated by mask. Intravenous fluid therapy and patient 
minoring during the anaesthesia are standard parts of a surgical procedure. The surgical field is shaved and prepared 
for surgery as standard.
